questions.question: At common law, duress traditionally required proof of:

options.A: Illegitimate pressure applied to the will of the contracting party by threats of any kind
options.B: Actual or threatened unlawful physical violence to the person of the contracting party (or their immediate family)
options.C: Economic pressure that deprived the contracting party of any meaningful choice
options.D: Threats of criminal prosecution made by the other contracting party

questions.explanation: At common law, the doctrine of duress was initially confined to actual or threatened unlawful physical violence to the person — duress of the person. A contract entered into as a result of such threats was voidable at the election of the threatened party. The narrow scope of the common law doctrine meant it was inadequate to address the range of improper pressures to which contracting parties could be subjected, leading to the development of the broader doctrine of economic duress.

questions.question: Economic duress was first recognised as a vitiating factor in English law in:

options.B: Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980] (PC)
options.C: Universe Tankships Inc of Monrovia v International Transport Workers Federation [1983] (HL)
options.D: The North Ocean Shipping Co Ltd v Hyundai Construction Co Ltd (The Atlantic Baron) [1979] (QBD)

questions.explanation: Economic duress was recognised as a vitiating factor in The Atlantic Baron [1979], where Mocatta J held that a threat to breach a shipbuilding contract unless additional payment was made constituted economic duress making the agreement to pay voidable. The case established the principle that illegitimate pressure that compels another party to enter a contract or make a payment may constitute actionable duress even in the absence of physical threats. However, in that case, the claim failed due to affirmation.

questions.question: The two core elements required to establish economic duress are:

options.A: Proof that the defendant made a profit from the transaction and that the claimant suffered a financial loss
options.B: Illegitimate pressure applied by the defendant, and the illegitimate pressure must have caused the claimant to enter or modify the contract (causation)
options.C: A threat of unlawful action and independent legal advice obtained by the claimant before capitulating
options.D: Proof that the claimant had no legal remedy at the time the pressure was applied and could not have resisted

questions.explanation: To establish economic duress, the claimant must prove: (1) illegitimate pressure — a threat or conduct that goes beyond what is permissible in commercial negotiations; and (2) causation — the illegitimate pressure must have caused the claimant to enter the contract or to make the variation in question. The 'but for' test of causation has been replaced by a broader inquiry: was the illegitimate pressure a significant cause inducing the claimant to act as they did? (DSND Subsea Ltd v Petroleum Geo-Services ASA [2000]).

questions.question: What is meant by 'illegitimate pressure' in the context of economic duress?

options.A: Any commercial pressure that results in a party agreeing to less favourable terms
options.B: Unlawful threats (e.g., threats to break a contract or commit a tort) or, in some circumstances, lawful acts used in an unconscionable manner
options.C: Any threat of legal proceedings, including the threat of a lawful claim
options.D: Commercial pressure that causes the other party to make a financial loss on the transaction

questions.explanation: Illegitimate pressure typically involves threats to do something unlawful — for example, a threat to breach a contract (Stilk v Myrick [1809] concerned this scenario from a different angle) or to commit a crime or tort. In some cases, a threat to do something lawful may still constitute illegitimate pressure if it is used in a morally or commercially unacceptable manner — 'lawful act duress.' The concept of illegitimacy focuses on whether the pressure was an improper use of coercion beyond the acceptable norms of commercial dealing.

questions.question: In Barton v Armstrong [1976] (PC), the Privy Council considered duress of the person. The Court held that:

options.A: Threats of physical violence must be the sole cause of the claimant's decision to enter the contract
options.B: Where threats of physical violence are made, the onus lies on the party who made the threats to prove they did not contribute to the claimant's decision to contract
options.C: A threat of physical violence vitiates consent only if the claimant had no other means of avoiding the threatened harm
options.D: Duress of the person requires proof that actual violence was inflicted on the claimant

questions.explanation: In Barton v Armstrong [1976], the Privy Council held that where duress of the person (threats of physical violence) is established, the onus shifts to the party who made the threats to prove that the threats did not in any way contribute to the claimant's decision to execute the deed. The threats need not be the sole or even the main cause — it is sufficient that they were a cause. This placed a demanding reversal of the normal burden of proof where threats to physical safety were involved.

questions.question: In Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980] (PC), Lord Scarman identified factors relevant to determining whether economic duress vitiates consent. Which of the following was NOT listed as a relevant factor?

options.A: Whether the claimant protested at the time
options.B: Whether the claimant had an alternative course open to them (e.g., an adequate legal remedy)
options.C: Whether the claimant was independently advised
options.D: Whether the claimant's financial position was weaker than the defendant's

questions.explanation: In Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980], Lord Scarman identified the following relevant factors for assessing whether commercial pressure amounts to duress: (1) whether the claimant protested; (2) whether there was any other course of action available (e.g., a legal remedy); (3) whether the claimant was independently advised; and (4) whether the claimant took steps to avoid the contract after it was made. Relative financial strength of the parties is not itself a listed criterion, though it may bear on the availability of alternatives.

questions.question: A contract induced by duress is:

options.B: Voidable — the victim may elect to rescind it, subject to the usual bars to rescission
options.C: Unenforceable — neither party may enforce it
options.D: Valid and enforceable unless a court orders otherwise

questions.explanation: A contract induced by duress is voidable, not void. The victim has the right to affirm the contract or to rescind it. Rescission is subject to the usual equitable bars: affirmation (where the victim confirms the contract with full knowledge of the duress), lapse of time, impossibility of restitution, and intervention of third party rights. The voidable nature of a contract for duress contrasts with cases of common mistake, which may render a contract void. In The Atlantic Baron [1979], the claimant's failure to protest promptly after the economic duress had ceased was held to amount to affirmation.

questions.question: In Universe Tankships Inc v International Transport Workers Federation [1983] (HL), the House of Lords considered economic duress arising from industrial action. The majority held that the critical question was whether the pressure exerted was:

options.A: Physical in nature — only physical threats could constitute duress at the time of the case
options.B: Legitimate or illegitimate — a threat to take lawful industrial action was not necessarily illegitimate
options.C: Financial in impact — the pressure must have caused a quantifiable financial loss
options.D: Deliberate — the pressuring party must have intended to harm the victim financially

questions.explanation: In Universe Tankships [1983], the House of Lords held that the coercion by the ITF (which 'blacked' the ship until a donation was made to their welfare fund) did constitute economic duress because the threat was unlawful — it constituted an actionable tort of intimidation. The Court confirmed that economic duress requires both: (1) pressure that is illegitimate; and (2) that the pressure compelled the claimant's act. The case is important for establishing that not all commercial or industrial pressure is illegitimate — the key is whether the act threatened is itself lawful or unlawful.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios is most likely to constitute economic duress?

options.A: A seller who refuses to lower their price during tough negotiation, knowing the buyer needs the goods urgently
options.B: A contractor who threatens to abandon a building project midway unless the client agrees to pay an additional sum, exploiting the client's vulnerability to walk-off at a critical time
options.C: A supplier who increases prices in line with rising raw material costs during a long-term supply contract
options.D: An employer who makes a reasonable redundancy offer to an employee facing financial pressure

questions.explanation: Economic duress involves illegitimate pressure that overrides the victim's free will. A threat to abandon a building project at a critical stage — exploiting the client's vulnerability and inability to find replacement contractors at short notice — is a threat to breach the contract. This constitutes illegitimate pressure beyond what is acceptable in commercial dealings. Option A involves hard but legitimate bargaining. Option C involves a lawful contractual right or reasonable commercial response. Option D may raise other issues but does not involve a threat to commit an unlawful act.

questions.question: The concept of 'lawful act duress' refers to:

options.A: Duress that is authorised by statute and therefore not actionable
options.B: The use of threats to perform (or not perform) an otherwise lawful act in a manner that is so unconscionable as to constitute illegitimate pressure
options.C: Duress by a party acting lawfully under a court order
options.D: Economic pressure applied in accordance with the terms of a valid contract

questions.explanation: Lawful act duress refers to cases where a party uses a threat to do something they are legally entitled to do, but in circumstances so unconscionable that the pressure constitutes illegitimate coercion. The law here is developing and uncertain. In Times Travel (UK) Ltd v Pakistan International Airlines Corp [2021] (UK SC), the Supreme Court confirmed that lawful act duress exists but is narrow — the making of a lawful demand does not ordinarily constitute duress even if the victim has no practical alternative to capitulating. Bad faith, unconscionability, or abuse of a dominant position may be relevant factors.
